itemListElement.name: GLS X167 Facelift GLS63 AMG (M177, 612 PS, 2023-present)

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned GLS63 AMG (M177) engine typically costs £7,000–£15,000 supply only depending on generation. Replacement engines for AMG models can cost £10,000–£20,000 depending on condition and warranty. A CPO GLS63 owner reported a complete engine replacement approved under warranty with the engine on backorder. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,500 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M177 is regarded as one of AMG's most reliable modern V8s, but its longevity depends on proper servicing. The most frequent and expensive problem encountered with mileage is oil separator failure, which often leads to rear main seal leaks. Other well‑documented issues include timing chain stretch, HPFP failure, valve cover gasket leaks, coolant ingress into cylinders, and 48V system issues. As one owner put it: 'Needless to say, this isn't the ownership experience I expected with a CPO AMG, but I'm thankful for warranty'.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑AMG GLS63?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil separator failure leading to rear main seal leaks (engine or transmission out job), coolant ingress causing misfires and complete engine failure (reported at 28,000 miles), timing chain stretch, HPFP failure, valve cover and turbo oil leaks, and 48V EQ Boost system issues. See the Era Map above for detailed advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept GLS63 (values £70,000–£120,000+ depending on year and mileage), a £8,000–£10,000 reconditioned engine is 8–15% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a solid example. The Mercedes scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code M177 is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The GLS63 AMG (2020–present) uses the 4.0‑litre M177 V8 biturbo engine with 48V EQ Boost. The pre‑facelift produces 603 bhp, while the facelift produces up to 612 PS. The M177 is developed and assembled by AMG in Affalterbach. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: How can I check for oil separator failure on the M177?

acceptedAnswer.text: With the engine idling, try to remove the oil fill cap. If there is excessive vacuum, the cap will be extremely difficult to remove due to strong suction. If you hear a whistling sound, try slightly loosening the oil cap — if the noise disappears or changes significantly, that is often a strong indication that the oil separator is failing. Both excessive positive pressure and excessive vacuum can damage seals and gaskets. If you notice oil dripping from the rear of the engine, oil around the transmission bellhousing, or a burning oil smell, have the system inspected immediately.

problems.title: Oil Separator Failure & Rear Main Seal Leak (M177)
problems.symptoms: Oil dripping from the rear of the engine, oil around the transmission bellhousing, burning oil smell, whistling or howling sound from the engine, unstable idle, oil cap difficult to remove due to strong suction.
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles — one of the most frequent and expensive problems encountered with mileage
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£4,000 (oil separator replacement and rear main seal — engine or transmission out)【PLACEHOLDER】
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if oil starvation leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice oil leaks or a whistling sound, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M177's oil separator is part of the crankcase ventilation system. When it fails, crankcase pressure becomes unbalanced. Excessive positive pressure forces oil past engine seals — the rear main seal is often the first to fail. Alternatively, excessive vacuum can pull seals inward, distorting or damaging them. A simple check: with the engine idling, try to remove the oil fill cap. If there is excessive vacuum, the cap will be extremely difficult to remove. If you hear a whistling sound, loosening the oil cap should change the noise. Both excessive positive pressure and excessive vacuum can damage seals, gaskets, and other engine components. Proper crankcase pressure balance is critical to the long‑term health of the M177.

problems.title: Coolant Ingress & Misfires (M177 — X167)
problems.symptoms: Intermittent misfires, coolant‑related faults, amber coolant warning with check engine light, coolant loss with no visible external leak, rough idle.
problems.typicalMileage: Can occur as early as 28,000 miles
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if coolant ingress leads to engine damage — complete engine replacement required)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you experience misfires or coolant warnings, stop driving and book an inspection immediately

problems.recommendation: A CPO GLS63 owner at 28,000 miles reported a transfer case seal leak, intermittent misfires, coolant‑related faults, and an amber coolant warning with a check engine light. Mercedes approved a complete engine replacement under warranty — the engine was backordered. This is a serious issue that can lead to complete engine failure. If you experience any misfires or coolant warnings, have the vehicle inspected immediately. The M177 is known for carbon buildup on intake valves, which can also cause misfires and rough idle.

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (M177)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or grinding noise from the front of the engine on cold start, engine warning light, loss of power, rough running.
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if chain snaps and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling or grinding, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: Timing chain problems are a known weakness of the M177, with symptoms including rattling or grinding noises. The timing chain cover can also develop leaks. Regular oil changes with high‑quality synthetic oil are essential to prevent timing chain wear. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately.

problems.title: High‑Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure (M177)
problems.symptoms: Hard starting, hesitation on acceleration, misfires, loss of power under load, engine warning light, reduced fuel economy.
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if fuel system failure leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if hesitation or hard starting occurs

problems.recommendation: HPFP failure is a known issue on the M177. Symptoms include hard starting, hesitation, and loss of power under load. If you experience any of these, have the fuel system inspected immediately. Replacing the pump before it fails completely can prevent damage to other fuel system components.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Valve Cover & Turbocharger (M177)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage around the valve covers or turbochargers, low oil warning.
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if oil starvation leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are one of the most frequent complaints with the M177, often stemming from poorly sealed gaskets. On turbocharged variants, oil from a valve cover gasket that contacts the turbocharger produces the characteristic burning smell. Oil leaks from the valve cover and turbocharger areas are common. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the system inspected promptly.

problems.title: 48V EQ Boost System Issues (M177)
problems.symptoms: Battery warning lights, stop‑start system failure, reduced power, 48V generator warnings, starting issues.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if warning lights appear

problems.recommendation: The GLS63 AMG features a 48V EQ Boost mild‑hybrid system with an integrated starter‑generator. Like all modern MHEV systems, it adds complexity and potential failure points. If you experience any battery or electrical warnings, have the vehicle inspected by a specialist with Mercedes MHEV experience.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.problem: Oil separator / rear main seal leak
rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair; engine or transmission out required
Catch it early; complete engine replacement may be required
Catch it early — far cheaper than engine replacement
Catch it before fuel system damage occurs
Requires specialist diagnostics; repairable without engine replacement
M177 failures are rare but catastrophic when they occur

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLS63 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using AMG‑approved oil
* Oil separator inspected with no signs of failure (check oil cap vacuum test)
* No oil leaks from the rear main seal or valve covers
* No coolant loss or misfire history
* Extended warranty in place — these cars are extraordinarily expensive to repair out of warranty
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — the M177 is unforgiving of neglect
* Any oil leaks — especially from the rear of the engine (rear main seal warning)
* Coolant loss or misfire history — coolant ingress can require a new engine
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the M177 can hide expensive faults
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 is the ultimate performance SUV — a 603‑bhp twin‑turbo V8, 48V mild‑hybrid assistance, and a chassis that defies its size. The M177 is one of AMG's most celebrated modern V8s, delivering up to 630 bhp with a blend of durability, performance, and the unmistakable AMG V8 soundtrack. However, it has significant vulnerabilities. The oil separator is the most frequent and expensive problem, often leading to rear main seal leaks. Coolant ingress has been reported on low‑mileage cars, requiring complete engine replacement. Timing chain stretch, HPFP failure, and oil leaks are also well‑documented. As one owner put it: "Needless to say, this isn't the ownership experience I expected with a CPO AMG, but I'm thankful for warranty". Buy on service history, prioritise a car with an extended warranty, and absolutely maintain a warranty. This is a brilliant SUV — but it demands deep pockets and careful ownership.

cta.label: → Compare GLS63 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Oil Separator Test — With the engine idling, try to remove the oil fill cap. Excessive suction or whistling indicates oil separator failure
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the rear of the engine (rear main seal) and valve covers
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates potential coolant ingress issues
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling from the front of the engine (timing chain warning)
* Service History — Regular oil changes with AMG‑approved oil are absolutely essential
* Misfire History — Any intermittent misfires or check engine lights? (coolant ingress warning)
* 48V System — Any battery or electrical warnings?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Oil separator, timing, HPFP, or coolant codes are most common

items.label: SECTION 8 — FAQ
items.value: 1. How much does a Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 engine replacement cost in the UK?

items.label: A reconditioned GLS63 AMG (M177) engine typically costs £7,000–£15,000 supply only depending on generation. Replacement engines for AMG models can cost £10,000–£20,000 depending on condition and warranty. A CPO GLS63 owner reported a complete engine replacement approved under warranty with the engine on backorder. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,500 at independent specialist rates. items.value: 2. Is the Mercedes‑AMG GLS63 engine reliable?

items.label: The M177 is regarded as one of AMG's most reliable modern V8s, but its longevity depends on proper servicing. The most frequent and expensive problem encountered with mileage is oil separator failure, which often leads to rear main seal leaks. Other well‑documented issues include timing chain stretch, HPFP failure, valve cover gasket leaks, coolant ingress into cylinders, and 48V system issues. Against a well‑kept GLS63 (values £70,000–£120,000+ depending on year and mileage), a £8,000–£10,000 reconditioned engine is 8–15% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a solid example. Given the GLS63's status as a flagship AMG SUV, engine replacement is almost always worth it for a good example — but only with a warranty.

If you hear a whistling sound, try slightly loosening the oil cap — if the noise disappears or changes significantly, that is often a strong indication that the oil separator is failing. Both excessive positive pressure and excessive vacuum can damage seals and gaskets. If you notice oil dripping from the rear of the engine, oil around the transmission bellhousing, or a burning oil smell, have the system inspected immediately.
